lines.text: Good evening, Madison. The clock reads {{CLOCK}}. We are live.
For those tuning in for the first time — welcome to Purge Radio, PRGE, the one night a year we get to say hello in public.
I'm Quinn Foley. I'm four whiskeys in already, and the night is young, and I have every intention of keeping both facts true until sunrise.
The Purge commenced at 19:00. We are {{ELAPSED}} in. Twelve hours total. You know the rules.
All law is suspended. All crime is legal. Government officials Level-10 and above are protected. Everyone else is fair game.
And yet, here you are. Listening to the radio instead of participating. I respect that. Truly.
We've got Caroline on weather, Holden on analysis, Tim keeping the signal hot, and field reports coming in from Tucker as the night develops.
But I want to start with something I've been noticing in the first hour, and maybe you have too.
It's quiet out there. Quieter than last year at this hour.
The city's not jumping yet. No sirens — well, there are sirens, but they're thin. The normal 7pm traffic sounds are just... absent.
Could be the weather. Could be economic factors — ammo prices are still brutal. Could be that people are just smarter about their barricading now.
Or could be that this year, Madison's taking a breath before it exhales.
Caroline, I'm gonna hand it to you for the weather picture. Give us tonight's forecast.
Thanks, Quinn. Caroline here. I've been tracking this system since Tuesday and I want to be very clear about what's coming.
We start clear. Forty-one degrees, southwest wind at eight miles per hour. Visibility is excellent — ten miles or better.
This is the 'decide what you're doing' hour. If you're going to move, this is when conditions are optimal for it.
Around 21:00, we start seeing clouds build in from the west. Wind picks up to twelve, gusts to eighteen.
By midnight, a cold front arrives. Temperature drops from thirty-seven to the low thirties. Rain develops, potentially transitioning to sleet by 01:00.
That system holds through the pre-dawn. We're looking at fog off Mendota around 03:00 to 04:00. Visibility drops to a mile and a half.
The fog clears by sunrise. Temperature bottoms out around twenty-one, clear skies, hard frost on everything.
So if you're hearing quietness right now, part of it might be that people are waiting. Waiting to see what the weather does.
The rain and cold will either suppress activity or funnel it indoors. Last year, we saw the kill count actually tick up during the fog phase.
People get claustrophobic. Visibility disappears. Things happen in the dark that wouldn't happen in clear conditions.
So we're looking at a night that's going to *transform* as we go. Clear and still now, then weather moves in.
Holden, before we get to the pre-Purge statistics, I want to ask you something.
In your view — and I know you think about this stuff — is the quiet we're hearing right now unusual?
Thanks, Quinn. Yes, I've been running the numbers against the same hour across the last five years, and it's statistically significant.
We're tracking approximately forty percent lower acoustic activity in the first hour compared to the 2164-2167 average.
That could indicate a few things. One: people are staying home in higher numbers. Two: people who are *going out* are being more cautious about making noise.
Three: there's a geographic concentration of activity that we're not catching yet because the violence is still establishing itself in preferred zones.
We know from 2168 data that the East Side typically lights up around 20:00 to 20:30. Capitol Square breach didn't happen until 21:40.
So it's entirely possible we're just in the settling phase. The calm before the metrics spike.
That's a comforting thought, Holden. Really warms the heart.
The data is what it is, Quinn. I'm just reading it.
But if I'm being asked for my *personal* take — and I probably shouldn't be, but I am — the quiet feels intentional this year.
There's a difference between calm and caution. This feels like the latter.
Okay. I'm going to try to not let that thought keep me awake for the next twelve hours.
Tim, we're still holding signal strong?
Rock solid. Backup power is at ninety-eight percent. Antenna rig is locked and oriented. We're broadcasting clean on the frequency.
First hour always runs quiet on the technical side. People aren't panicked yet, so they're not trying to jam us.
That'll change. But right now, we're golden.
And if someone *does* try to jam us?
We relocate the antenna, move the rig, and we're back in four to five minutes. Did it a dozen times in testing. We can do it in the field.
I appreciate that confidence, Tim. Truly.
Alright, so here's what we know as we settle into this night. The Purge is live. The city is quiet. The weather is about to get interesting.
And we're going to be here, documenting it, analyzing it, maybe making bad jokes about it, until 07:00 tomorrow.
If you're listening because you're home and you're scared, we see you. You're not alone.
If you're listening because you're home and you're safe and you just want company, we see that too.
If you're listening for other reasons, well, we don't ask questions on this station.
The phones are open for callers. Tucker's standing by for field reports. Caroline's monitoring the weather. Holden's running the numbers.
And I'm going to pour whiskey number five and we're going to get through this together.
Quinn, before we move on, I want to note that I've been watching the Mendota water temperature.
As of 18:55 — just before we went live — it was holding at thirty-four degrees.
If anyone's considering water-based movement tonight, I'm telling you now: survivability window at that temperature is approximately twenty minutes.
Most people don't make it that far. Just wanted that on record.
Caroline's correct. Lake drownings account for approximately three to four percent of total Purge casualties in Madison, but the survival rate is nearly zero.
The cold shock response triggers within two minutes. Panic sets in. Most people aspirate water before rescue is even possible.
So if you've been thinking about the lake as an escape route, don't.
We're just hitting you with the cheerful facts right out of the gate, aren't we?
This is Purge Radio. We don't sugarcoat.
Got a text incoming. Someone on the South Side asking if we're taking callers yet.
Put them through. We've got time.
But before we do, let me run through tonight's lineup real quick.
We've got news and weather through the top of the hour. Then we're shifting to sports and neighborhood breakdown with Holden and Caroline.
At 20:30, Tucker's going to start checking in from the field. Updates every thirty minutes or as conditions warrant.
Nick's music block runs from 21:00 through 02:00. Solid punk all night. No Springsteen, no country, no exceptions.
Sister Marigold's wellness meditations run every two hours starting at 22:00. If you need to hear her voice at three in the morning, she'll be here.
And I'll be anchoring through the night, trying to make sense of whatever's happening out there and failing in entertaining ways.
I want to add something to the neighborhood forecast, if I can.
Based on 2168 data and the economic shifts we've seen in the past year, I'm expecting the East Side to heat up around 20:00 to 20:30.
That zone is consistently the hottest in Madison — eighteen percent of total kills last year, fourth year running.
Atwood, Schenk's Corners, the Northside of that corridor — those neighborhoods are primed.
Capitol Square is fortified this year like it wasn't last year, so I expect less breach attempts. But State Street — the State Street ruins are a wildcard.
No organized presence. No barricades. Just the urban skeleton and whoever decides to move through it.
And the weather's going to shape all of that. Wind pushing out of the southwest now means the smoke from fires will drift toward the northwest side.
When the cold front moves in and the wind shifts north and west, that changes the whole geometry.
Fire spread patterns, visibility for people moving through affected areas, even tactical positioning — it all shifts with the weather.
So what you're saying is that the atmosphere — literally, the actual weather — is a factor in how many people die tonight.
That's always true. But yes, explicitly, tonight it will be a measurable factor.
And we'll be tracking it. Tim, we ready for that first caller?
Line's open. South Side, like I said. Caller's name is — they didn't give a name. Just 'South Side.'
We love it. Put them on.
You're on Purge Radio. What's happening down there?
I should mention — and I'm sorry to talk over you, but this is important — that we're not going to be able to help if you need active assistance.
We're not connected to any emergency services. We're radio. We're a voice. That's all we are.
But if you want to talk, we can do that. Sometimes talking helps.
What's your situation?
While we're waiting for them to respond, let me pull up one more statistic that's been sitting with me.
The three neighborhoods that *didn't* spike last year — North Side, Vilas Park, parts of the West Side — they all had something in common.
Either organized community presence, private security, or in one case, a territorial agreement we still haven't figured out.
The difference between a bad Purge and a survivable one is often just organization.
Which neighborhoods are you living in, if people are listening?
If you're in Atwood or East Side generally, you're in the highest-risk zone. Get ready for activity by 20:30.
If you're on the South or West Side with organized barricades, you've got better odds. Stay vigilant.
If you're in areas with private security or natural barriers, you're in the relative safe zones. That doesn't mean safe, by the way. It means safer.
And if the caller from the South Side is still with us, I'm genuinely curious what brings them to the radio at this hour.
They're still on the line. Just not talking yet. I can hear breathing.
That's okay. Take your time. We've got twelve hours.
This is the deal with Purge Radio — we're not here to rush anyone. If you need silence and a voice on the other end, that's what we do.
I'm going to use this moment to circle back to the eerie quiet we started with.
In 2165, we had a similar first hour. Acoustic data showed a thirty-five percent reduction in baseline ambient violence indicators.
That year, the kill count ended up being the lowest on record for Madison — 194 total.
So quiet first hours don't necessarily correlate with quiet nights. Sometimes they mean something is being planned. Organized. Delayed for tactical reasons.
This could be one of those years.
So you're saying we might be sitting on top of something and we just don't know it yet.
I'm saying the quiet is data. And data without context can mean almost anything.
The weather will help us understand it. Once the conditions change, we'll see how people respond.
My prediction is that activity ticks up significantly once the wind picks up and the cloud cover moves in. Darkness makes people bolder.
Caller from the South Side is going to disconnect. They're doing that thing where they can't quite speak but they needed to be less alone.
That's fair. Hang in there. We'll be here. All night.
So here's where we are at the top of the opening hour. {{ELAPSED}} since the Purge commenced.
The city is quiet in a way that might be calm or might be ominous. Weather's about to shift. The East Side is a ticking clock.
And we're going to be here, tracking it all, until the sun comes up.
This is Quinn Foley on Purge Radio. We'll be right back.

tickerItems.text: Madison Purge commenced at 19:00. All law suspended until 07:00 tomorrow. Level-10+ officials protected. Stay home or barricade.
Lake Mendota water temperature 34°F. Survivability window approximately 20 minutes. Do not attempt crossing.
East Side heating up projected for 20:30. Atwood / Schenk's Corners residents prepare for activity.
PurgeShield paneling installed wrong? Check orientation now. Call 608-555-SAFE for emergency repositioning.
Capitol Square fortified this year. State Street ruins undefended. Weather shifting to cold front by 21:00.
South Side caller reports barricades in place, supplies adequate, staying home. Phones open all night.
